Back to Blog

A Rigorous Derivation of Policy Gradient, Actor-Critic, PPO, RLHF, and GRPO

December 19, 2025 (6mo ago)

Introduction

Reinforcement learning (RL) provides a principled framework for optimizing sequential decision-making policies in stochastic environments. In this context, an agent interacts with a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and seeks to maximize an expected cumulative reward.

Policy gradient methods form a cornerstone of modern deep RL, allowing the direct optimization of parameterized stochastic policies. These methods provide:

  • A formal gradient-based procedure for maximizing expected return,

  • Flexibility to handle high-dimensional and continuous action spaces,

  • Compatibility with function approximation using neural networks.

Despite their generality, vanilla policy gradient algorithms often suffer from high variance and unstable updates. Actor-critic methods mitigate these issues by introducing a learned value function to estimate the advantage, while TRPO and PPO further stabilize policy updates by controlling the effective step size in policy space.

Large-scale applications, such as training language models to follow instructions, introduce additional challenges:

  • Rewards are often not directly observed, requiring learned reward models,

  • Value estimation is difficult or costly for high-dimensional discrete sequences,

  • Stability and sample efficiency are crucial due to the scale of data and model size.

Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) addresses these challenges by combining:

  1. A pretrained or fine-tuned policy for initialization,

  2. A reward model learned from human preferences,

  3. Stable policy optimization methods such as PPO.

Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) extends RLHF by removing the critic entirely and leveraging group-relative baselines, providing a more stable and computationally efficient method suitable for large-scale language model alignment.

This document provides a comprehensive and rigorous treatment of:

  • Policy gradient methods and the advantage formulation,

  • Actor-critic algorithms and temporal-difference learning,

  • Theoretical foundations including the performance difference lemma,

  • Trust-region and proximal optimization methods (TRPO, PPO),

  • Reinforcement learning from human feedback and group-relative approaches (RLHF, GRPO).

The exposition emphasizes formal derivations, clear definitions, and detailed algorithmic insights, enabling implementation and practical understanding from first principles.

Foundations and Markov Decision Processes

This section establishes the mathematical framework underlying reinforcement learning. We adopt a measure-theoretic perspective in order to make expectations, probability distributions, and gradients well-defined. All subsequent derivations build on these definitions.

Measurable Spaces and Probability Kernels

Let

(S,FS)(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{F}_{\mathcal{S}})(S,FS​)

and

(A,FA)(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{F}_{\mathcal{A}})(A,FA​)

denote measurable state and action spaces, respectively. We assume that both spaces are standard Borel spaces, which guarantees the existence of regular conditional probabilities.

::: definition Definition 1 (Probability Kernel). *A probability kernel from X\mathcal{X}X to Y\mathcal{Y}Y is a mapping

K:X×FY→[0,1]K : \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{F}_{\mathcal{Y}} \to [0,1]K:X×FY​→[0,1]

such that:*

  1. For each fixed x∈Xx \in \mathcal{X}x∈X, K(⋅∣x)K(\cdot \mid x)K(⋅∣x) is a probability measure on Y\mathcal{Y}Y.

  2. For each measurable set B⊆YB \subseteq \mathcal{Y}B⊆Y, the function x↦K(B∣x)x \mapsto K(B \mid x)x↦K(B∣x) is measurable. :::

Probability kernels allow us to define stochastic transitions and policies in a mathematically precise way.

Markov Decision Processes

::: definition Definition 2 (Discounted Markov Decision Process). *A discounted Markov Decision Process (MDP) is a tuple

M=(S,A,P,r,ρ,γ),\mathcal{M} = (\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, r, \rho, \gamma),M=(S,A,P,r,ρ,γ),

where:*

  • S\mathcal{S}S is the state space;

  • A\mathcal{A}A is the action space;

  • P(⋅∣s,a)P(\cdot \mid s,a)P(⋅∣s,a) is a probability kernel on S\mathcal{S}S, called the transition kernel;

  • r:S×A→Rr : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \to \mathbb{R}r:S×A→R is the reward function;

  • ρ\rhoρ is a probability measure on S\mathcal{S}S, called the initial state distribution;

  • γ∈(0,1)\gamma \in (0,1)γ∈(0,1) is the discount factor. :::

The discount factor ensures that infinite-horizon returns are well-defined. We assume throughout that the reward function is bounded, i.e.,

∣r(s,a)∣≤Rmax⁡<∞,∀(s,a)∈S×A.|r(s,a)| \leq R_{\max} < \infty, \quad \forall (s,a) \in \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A}.∣r(s,a)∣≤Rmax​<∞,∀(s,a)∈S×A.

Policies

::: definition Definition 3 (Stochastic Policy). *A stochastic policy is a probability kernel

π(⋅∣s)\pi(\cdot \mid s)π(⋅∣s)

from S\mathcal{S}S to A\mathcal{A}A.* :::

In this document, we focus on differentiable parametric policies.

::: definition Definition 4 (Parametric Policy). *A parametric policy is a family of probability kernels

{πθ(⋅∣s)}θ∈Rd\{\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s)\}_{\theta \in \mathbb{R}^d}{πθ​(⋅∣s)}θ∈Rd​

such that for every (s,a)(s,a)(s,a), the mapping θ↦πθ(a∣s)\theta \mapsto \pi_\theta(a \mid s)θ↦πθ​(a∣s) is differentiable.* :::

Typical examples include softmax policies in discrete action spaces and Gaussian policies in continuous action spaces.

Trajectories and Induced Probability Measures

A policy interacting with an MDP induces a probability distribution over infinite trajectories.

::: definition Definition 5 (Trajectory). *A trajectory is an infinite sequence

τ=(s0,a0,s1,a1,s2,a2,… ),\tau = (s_0, a_0, s_1, a_1, s_2, a_2, \dots),τ=(s0​,a0​,s1​,a1​,s2​,a2​,…),

where st∈Ss_t \in \mathcal{S}st​∈S and at∈Aa_t \in \mathcal{A}at​∈A.* :::

Given a policy πθ\pi_\thetaπθ​, the joint distribution over trajectories is defined by

Pθ(τ)=ρ(s0)∏t=0∞πθ(at∣st)P(st+1∣st,at).\mathbb{P}_\theta(\tau) = \rho(s_0) \prod_{t=0}^\infty \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t).Pθ​(τ)=ρ(s0​)t=0∏∞​πθ​(at​∣st​)P(st+1​∣st​,at​).

This expression should be interpreted as defining a probability measure on the infinite product space

(S×A)N.(\mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A})^{\mathbb{N}}.(S×A)N.

Return and Discounted Rewards

::: definition Definition 6 (Return). *The (discounted) return of a trajectory τ\tauτ is defined as

R(τ)=∑t=0∞γtr(st,at).R(\tau) = \sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t).R(τ)=t=0∑∞​γtr(st​,at​).

:::

Since rewards are bounded and γ<1\gamma < 1γ<1, the series converges absolutely almost surely.

Policy Objective

The goal of reinforcement learning is to find a policy that maximizes the expected discounted return.

::: definition Definition 7 (Expected Return). *The performance of a policy πθ\pi_\thetaπθ​ is defined as

J(θ)=Eτ∼Pθ[R(τ)].J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \mathbb{P}_\theta} \left[ R(\tau) \right].J(θ)=Eτ∼Pθ​​[R(τ)].

:::

This expectation is taken jointly over:

  • the initial state s0∼ρs_0 \sim \rhos0​∼ρ,

  • the stochastic policy πθ\pi_\thetaπθ​,

  • the environment dynamics PPP.

The dependence of J(θ)J(\theta)J(θ) on θ\thetaθ is entirely through the policy.

Conditional Expectations and Markov Structure

A crucial property of MDPs is conditional independence.

::: proposition Proposition 1 (Markov Property). Conditioned on (st,at)(s_t, a_t)(st​,at​), the next state st+1s_{t+1}st+1​ is independent of all past states and actions. :::

This property allows us to define value functions and action-value functions in terms of conditional expectations.

State-Value and Action-Value Functions

::: definition Definition 8 (State-Value Function). *For a given policy π\piπ, the state-value function is

Vπ(s)=Eπ[∑t=0∞γtr(st,at)  |  s0=s].V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t r(s_t,a_t) \;\middle|\; s_0 = s \right].Vπ(s)=Eπ​[t=0∑∞​γtr(st​,at​)​s0​=s].

:::

::: definition Definition 9 (Action-Value Function). *The action-value function is

Qπ(s,a)=Eπ[∑t=0∞γtr(st,at)  |  s0=s,a0=a].Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t r(s_t,a_t) \;\middle|\; s_0 = s, a_0 = a \right].Qπ(s,a)=Eπ​[t=0∑∞​γtr(st​,at​)​s0​=s,a0​=a].

:::

Both expectations are taken with respect to the trajectory distribution induced by π\piπ and PPP.

Bellman Equations

The value functions satisfy the Bellman equations.

::: lemma Lemma 1 (Bellman Expectation Equation). *For all s∈Ss \in \mathcal{S}s∈S,

Vπ(s)=Ea∼π(⋅∣s)[r(s,a)+γEs′∼P(⋅∣s,a)[Vπ(s′)]].V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)} \left[ r(s,a) + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{s' \sim P(\cdot \mid s,a)} [V^\pi(s')] \right].Vπ(s)=Ea∼π(⋅∣s)​[r(s,a)+γEs′∼P(⋅∣s,a)​[Vπ(s′)]].

:::

::: lemma Lemma 2 (Bellman Equation for QπQ^\piQπ). *For all (s,a)(s,a)(s,a),

Qπ(s,a)=r(s,a)+γEs′∼P(⋅∣s,a)[Ea′∼π(⋅∣s′)[Qπ(s′,a′)]].Q^\pi(s,a) = r(s,a) + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{s' \sim P(\cdot \mid s,a)} \left[ \mathbb{E}_{a' \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s')} [Q^\pi(s',a')] \right].Qπ(s,a)=r(s,a)+γEs′∼P(⋅∣s,a)​[Ea′∼π(⋅∣s′)​[Qπ(s′,a′)]].

:::

Why This Formalism Matters

The explicit construction of probability measures and expectations is essential for:

  • justifying differentiation under the expectation sign,

  • proving unbiasedness of gradient estimators,

  • understanding variance reduction techniques,

  • analyzing trust-region and preference-based objectives.

In the next section, we will build on this foundation to derive the policy gradient theorem by differentiating the expected return with respect to policy parameters.

Policy Gradient Methods

In this section we derive the policy gradient theorem from first principles. Our goal is to compute the gradient of the expected return

J(θ)=Eτ∼Pθ[R(τ)]J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \mathbb{P}_\theta}[R(\tau)]J(θ)=Eτ∼Pθ​​[R(τ)]

with respect to the policy parameters θ\thetaθ.

Throughout this section, all expectations are taken with respect to the trajectory distribution induced by the policy πθ\pi_\thetaπθ​ and the MDP dynamics, unless otherwise stated.

Differentiating Expectations over Trajectories

Recall that

J(θ)=∫R(τ) dPθ(τ).J(\theta)=\int R(\tau)\, d\mathbb{P}_\theta(\tau).J(θ)=∫R(τ)dPθ​(τ).

We assume that:

  • πθ(a∣s)\pi_\theta(a \mid s)πθ​(a∣s) is differentiable in θ\thetaθ,

  • rewards are bounded,

  • differentiation and integration can be interchanged.

Under these assumptions, we may write

∇θJ(θ)=∇θ∫R(τ) dPθ(τ)=∫R(τ) ∇θdPθ(τ).\nabla_\theta J(\theta)=\nabla_\theta\int R(\tau)\, d\mathbb{P}_\theta(\tau)=\int R(\tau)\, \nabla_\theta d\mathbb{P}_\theta(\tau).∇θ​J(θ)=∇θ​∫R(τ)dPθ​(τ)=∫R(τ)∇θ​dPθ​(τ).

Log-Derivative Trick

Using the identity

∇θp(x)=p(x)∇θlog⁡p(x),\nabla_\theta p(x) = p(x)\nabla_\theta \log p(x),∇θ​p(x)=p(x)∇θ​logp(x),

we obtain

∇θJ(θ)=∫R(τ) ∇θlog⁡Pθ(τ) dPθ(τ)=Eτ∼Pθ[R(τ)∇θlog⁡Pθ(τ)].\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \int R(\tau)\, \nabla_\theta \log \mathbb{P}_\theta(\tau)\, d\mathbb{P}_\theta(\tau) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \mathbb{P}_\theta} \left[ R(\tau)\nabla_\theta \log \mathbb{P}_\theta(\tau) \right].∇θ​J(θ)=∫R(τ)∇θ​logPθ​(τ)dPθ​(τ)=Eτ∼Pθ​​[R(τ)∇θ​logPθ​(τ)].

This identity is the foundation of all policy gradient methods.

Structure of the Trajectory Distribution

Recall that the trajectory probability factorizes as

Pθ(τ)=ρ(s0)∏t=0∞πθ(at∣st)P(st+1∣st,at).\mathbb{P}_\theta(\tau) = \rho(s_0) \prod_{t=0}^\infty \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t,a_t).Pθ​(τ)=ρ(s0​)t=0∏∞​πθ​(at​∣st​)P(st+1​∣st​,at​).

Only the policy terms depend on θ\thetaθ. Therefore,

log⁡Pθ(τ)=log⁡ρ(s0)+∑t=0∞log⁡πθ(at∣st)+∑t=0∞log⁡P(st+1∣st,at).\log \mathbb{P}_\theta(\tau) = \log \rho(s_0) + \sum_{t=0}^\infty \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) + \sum_{t=0}^\infty \log P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t,a_t).logPθ​(τ)=logρ(s0​)+t=0∑∞​logπθ​(at​∣st​)+t=0∑∞​logP(st+1​∣st​,at​).

Taking the gradient yields

∇θlog⁡Pθ(τ)=∑t=0∞∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st).\nabla_\theta \log \mathbb{P}_\theta(\tau)= \sum_{t=0}^\infty \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t).∇θ​logPθ​(τ)=t=0∑∞​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​).

Substituting into the previous expression gives

∇θJ(θ)=E[R(τ)∑t=0∞∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)].\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E} \left[ R(\tau) \sum_{t=0}^\infty \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \right].∇θ​J(θ)=E[R(τ)t=0∑∞​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)].

This expression is correct but suffers from extremely high variance.

Causality and Reward-to-Go

We now exploit the temporal structure of the MDP to reduce variance.

Write the total return as R(τ)=∑k=0∞γkr(sk,ak).R(\tau) =\sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r(s_k,a_k).R(τ)=∑k=0∞​γkr(sk​,ak​).

Substituting into the gradient expression: ∇θJ(θ)=E[∑t=0∞∑k=0∞γkr(sk,ak)∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)].\nabla_\theta J(\theta)= \mathbb{E} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r(s_k,a_k) \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \right].∇θ​J(θ)=E[∑t=0∞​∑k=0∞​γkr(sk​,ak​)∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)].

We now separate the inner sum into two parts: ∑k=0∞=∑k=0t−1+∑k=t∞.\sum_{k=0}^\infty= \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} + \sum_{k=t}^\infty.∑k=0∞​=∑k=0t−1​+∑k=t∞​.

Causality Argument

::: lemma Lemma 3 (Causality). *For k<tk < tk<t,

E[γkr(sk,ak)∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)]=0.\mathbb{E} \left[ \gamma^k r(s_k,a_k) \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \right] = 0.E[γkr(sk​,ak​)∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)]=0.

:::

::: proof Proof. For k<tk < tk<t, the reward r(sk,ak)r(s_k,a_k)r(sk​,ak​) depends only on (s0,a0,…,sk,ak)(s_0,a_0,\dots,s_k,a_k)(s0​,a0​,…,sk​,ak​), while ∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​) depends only on (st,at)(s_t,a_t)(st​,at​).

By the Markov property, conditioned on sts_tst​, the past is independent of ata_tat​. Moreover,

Eat∼πθ(⋅∣st)[∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)]=∇θ∫πθ(at∣st) dat=∇θ1=0.\mathbb{E}_{a_t \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s_t)} \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \right] = \nabla_\theta \int \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, da_t = \nabla_\theta 1 = 0.Eat​∼πθ​(⋅∣st​)​[∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)]=∇θ​∫πθ​(at​∣st​)dat​=∇θ​1=0.

 ◻ :::

Reward-to-Go

By the causality lemma, only rewards from time ttt onward contribute. Define the reward-to-go:

Gt=∑k=t∞γk−tr(sk,ak).G_t = \sum_{k=t}^\infty \gamma^{k-t} r(s_k,a_k).Gt​=k=t∑∞​γk−tr(sk​,ak​).

Then the policy gradient becomes

∇θJ(θ)=E[∑t=0∞∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)Gt].\nabla_\theta J(\theta)= \mathbb{E} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) G_t \right].∇θ​J(θ)=E[t=0∑∞​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)Gt​].

This estimator has strictly lower variance than using the full return.

Action-Value Function Interpretation

The reward-to-go is a random variable. We now express it in terms of a conditional expectation.

::: definition Definition 10 (Action-Value Function). *

Qπθ(s,a)=Eπθ[G0  |  s0=s,a0=a].Q^{\pi_\theta}(s,a)= \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta} \left[ G_0 \;\middle|\; s_0 = s, a_0 = a \right].Qπθ​(s,a)=Eπθ​​[G0​∣s0​=s,a0​=a].

:::

By time-shift invariance,

E[Gt∣st,at]=Qπθ(st,at).\mathbb{E}[G_t \mid s_t, a_t] = Q^{\pi_\theta}(s_t,a_t).E[Gt​∣st​,at​]=Qπθ​(st​,at​).

Taking conditional expectation inside the sum yields the policy gradient theorem.

Policy Gradient Theorem

::: theorem Theorem 1 (Policy Gradient Theorem). *

∇θJ(θ)=Eπθ[∑t=0∞∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)Qπθ(st,at)].\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) Q^{\pi_\theta}(s_t,a_t) \right].∇θ​J(θ)=Eπθ​​[t=0∑∞​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)Qπθ​(st​,at​)].

:::

This expression depends only on quantities local in time.

Monte Carlo Estimation

In practice, the expectation is approximated using sampled trajectories

{τ(i)}i=1N∼Pθ\{\tau^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^N \sim \mathbb{P}_\theta{τ(i)}i=1N​∼Pθ​

.

The Monte Carlo estimator is

∇θJ^=1N∑i=1N∑t=0Ti∇θlog⁡πθ(at(i)∣st(i))Gt(i).\widehat{\nabla_\theta J}= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \sum_{t=0}^{T_i} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t^{(i)} \mid s_t^{(i)}) G_t^{(i)}.∇θ​J​=N1​i=1∑N​t=0∑Ti​​∇θ​logπθ​(at(i)​∣st(i)​)Gt(i)​.

By the law of large numbers, this estimator converges almost surely to the true gradient as N→∞N \to \inftyN→∞.

Variance Considerations

Although unbiased, the estimator suffers from:

  • long-horizon variance due to GtG_tGt​,

  • sensitivity to reward scaling,

  • poor sample efficiency.

These issues motivate the introduction of baselines and advantage functions, which we study in the next section.

Baselines and Advantage Functions

The policy gradient theorem expresses the gradient of the expected return as

∇θJ(θ)=Eπθ[∑t=0∞∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)Qπθ(st,at)].\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) Q^{\pi_\theta}(s_t,a_t) \right].∇θ​J(θ)=Eπθ​​[t=0∑∞​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)Qπθ​(st​,at​)].

While this estimator is unbiased, it typically exhibits high variance. In this section, we introduce baselines, prove that they do not bias the gradient, and show that the resulting formulation naturally leads to the advantage function.

Baselines and Unbiasedness

We begin by formalizing the notion of a baseline.

::: definition Definition 11 (Baseline). *A baseline is a measurable function

b:S→Rb : \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}b:S→R

that depends only on the state.* :::

Baselines are used to reduce variance in gradient estimates without altering their expectation.

Baseline Invariance Theorem

::: theorem Theorem 2 (Baseline Invariance). *For any measurable function b:S→Rb : \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}b:S→R,

Eπθ[∑t=0∞∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st) b(st)]=0.\mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, b(s_t) \right] = 0.Eπθ​​[t=0∑∞​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)b(st​)]=0.

:::

::: proof Proof. It suffices to prove that each term in the sum has zero expectation. Fix ttt. Then

E[∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st) b(st)]=Est[b(st)Eat∼πθ(⋅∣st)[∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)]].\mathbb{E} \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, b(s_t) \right] = \mathbb{E}_{s_t} \left[ b(s_t) \mathbb{E}_{a_t \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s_t)} \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \right] \right].E[∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)b(st​)]=Est​​[b(st​)Eat​∼πθ​(⋅∣st​)​[∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)]].

Since

Eat∼πθ(⋅∣st)[∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)]=∇θ∫πθ(at∣st) dat=∇θ1=0,\mathbb{E}_{a_t \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s_t)} \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \right] = \nabla_\theta \int \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, da_t = \nabla_\theta 1 = 0,Eat​∼πθ​(⋅∣st​)​[∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)]=∇θ​∫πθ​(at​∣st​)dat​=∇θ​1=0,

the result follows. ◻ :::

Baseline-Augmented Policy Gradient

Using the baseline invariance theorem, we may subtract any baseline from the action-value function inside the policy gradient:

∇θJ(θ)=Eπθ[∑t=0∞∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)(Qπθ(st,at)−b(st))].\nabla_\theta J(\theta)= \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \bigl( Q^{\pi_\theta}(s_t,a_t) - b(s_t) \bigr) \right].∇θ​J(θ)=Eπθ​​[t=0∑∞​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)(Qπθ​(st​,at​)−b(st​))].

This expression remains unbiased for any choice of bbb.

State-Value Function as a Baseline

A particularly important choice of baseline is the state-value function.

::: definition Definition 12 (State-Value Function). *

Vπ(s)=Ea∼π(⋅∣s)[Qπ(s,a)].V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)} \left[ Q^{\pi}(s,a) \right].Vπ(s)=Ea∼π(⋅∣s)​[Qπ(s,a)].

:::

This definition is equivalent to the conditional expectation of the return given the state:

Vπ(s)=Eπ[G0  |  s0=s].V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ G_0 \;\middle|\; s_0 = s \right].Vπ(s)=Eπ​[G0​∣s0​=s].

Advantage Function

::: definition Definition 13 (Advantage Function). *The advantage function is defined as

Aπ(s,a)=Qπ(s,a)−Vπ(s).A^\pi(s,a) = Q^\pi(s,a) - V^\pi(s).Aπ(s,a)=Qπ(s,a)−Vπ(s).

:::

The advantage measures how much better (or worse) an action is compared to the average action at a given state under policy π\piπ.

Policy Gradient in Advantage Form

Using the state-value function as a baseline yields

∇θJ(θ)=Eπθ[∑t=0∞∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)Aπθ(st,at)].\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) A^{\pi_\theta}(s_t,a_t) \right].∇θ​J(θ)=Eπθ​​[t=0∑∞​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)Aπθ​(st​,at​)].

This is the standard form used in modern policy gradient methods.

Why the Advantage is the Correct Signal

The advantage formulation has several important properties:

  • Ea∼π(⋅∣s)[Aπ(s,a)]=0\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)}[A^\pi(s,a)] = 0Ea∼π(⋅∣s)​[Aπ(s,a)]=0 for all sss,

  • actions better than average produce positive updates,

  • actions worse than average produce negative updates,

  • variance is minimized among all state-dependent baselines.

In particular, the state-value function is the optimal baseline in the mean-squared sense.

Optimal Baseline (Variance Minimization)

Consider a fixed state sss. The variance of the gradient estimator

g(a)=∇θlog⁡πθ(a∣s)(Qπ(s,a)−b(s))g(a) = \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) (Q^\pi(s,a) - b(s))g(a)=∇θ​logπθ​(a∣s)(Qπ(s,a)−b(s))

is minimized when

b(s)=Vπ(s).b(s) = V^\pi(s).b(s)=Vπ(s).

This result justifies the use of the advantage function from a variance-reduction perspective.

Sample-Based Estimation

In practice, QπQ^\piQπ and VπV^\piVπ are unknown and must be estimated.

Given a sampled trajectory, we approximate:

Qπ(st,at)≈Gt,Vπ(st)≈V^(st).Q^\pi(s_t,a_t) \approx G_t, \qquad V^\pi(s_t) \approx \hat{V}(s_t).Qπ(st​,at​)≈Gt​,Vπ(st​)≈V^(st​).

The resulting empirical advantage estimate is

A^t=Gt−V^(st).\hat{A}_t = G_t - \hat{V}(s_t).A^t​=Gt​−V^(st​).

This estimator is unbiased if V^=Vπ\hat{V} = V^\piV^=Vπ, and biased otherwise.

Bias-Variance Tradeoff

Using an approximate value function introduces bias but dramatically reduces variance. This tradeoff is at the heart of modern reinforcement learning algorithms.

The next section formalizes this idea through actor-critic methods and temporal-difference learning.

Actor-Critic Methods and Temporal-Difference Learning

Policy gradient methods estimate

∇θJ(θ)=Eπθ[∑t=0∞∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)Aπθ(st,at)].\nabla_\theta J(\theta) =\mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) A^{\pi_\theta}(s_t,a_t) \right].∇θ​J(θ)=Eπθ​​[t=0∑∞​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)Aπθ​(st​,at​)].

However, computing the true advantage function requires knowledge of the environment dynamics and the policy’s long-term behavior. Actor-critic methods address this by introducing a parametric approximation of the value function, called the critic, and using it to construct a low-variance estimator of the policy gradient.

Separation of Roles: Actor and Critic

Actor-critic methods maintain two sets of parameters:

  • Actor parameters θ\thetaθ, defining the policy πθ\pi_\thetaπθ​

  • Critic parameters www, defining a value function approximation Vw≈VπθV_w \approx V^{\pi_\theta}Vw​≈Vπθ​

The actor improves the policy by gradient ascent, while the critic evaluates the current policy by solving an approximate Bellman equation.

Value Function Approximation

::: definition Definition 14 (Parametric Value Function). *Let

Vw:S→RV_w : \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}Vw​:S→R

be a differentiable function approximator parameterized by w∈Rmw \in \mathbb{R}^mw∈Rm, intended to approximate the true state-value function VπθV^{\pi_\theta}Vπθ​.* :::

The critic is trained to minimize the discrepancy between its predictions and the Bellman target.

Bellman Equation

The true value function satisfies the Bellman expectation equation:

Vπ(s)=Ea∼π(⋅∣s)[r(s,a)+γEs′∼P(⋅∣s,a)Vπ(s′)].V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)} \left[ r(s,a) + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{s' \sim P(\cdot \mid s,a)} V^\pi(s') \right].Vπ(s)=Ea∼π(⋅∣s)​[r(s,a)+γEs′∼P(⋅∣s,a)​Vπ(s′)].

This fixed-point equation is the foundation of value-based learning.

Temporal-Difference Learning

Rather than estimating VπV^\piVπ via Monte Carlo returns, actor-critic methods use bootstrapped updates.

::: definition Definition 15 (Temporal-Difference Error). *The temporal-difference (TD) error at time ttt is defined as

δt=rt+γVw(st+1)−Vw(st).\delta_t = r_t + \gamma V_w(s_{t+1}) - V_w(s_t).δt​=rt​+γVw​(st+1​)−Vw​(st​).

:::

The TD error measures the instantaneous inconsistency of the critic with respect to the Bellman equation.

Critic Objective

The critic parameters www are updated by minimizing the mean-squared TD error:

Lcritic(w)=Eπθ[δt2].\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{critic}}(w) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta} \left[ \delta_t^2 \right].Lcritic​(w)=Eπθ​​[δt2​].

The gradient descent update is

w←w−αw∇wδt2.w \leftarrow w - \alpha_w \nabla_w \delta_t^2.w←w−αw​∇w​δt2​.

TD Error as an Advantage Estimator

The TD error is not equal to the advantage function, but its expectation recovers it.

::: theorem Theorem 3 (TD Error and Advantage). *If Vw=VπθV_w = V^{\pi_\theta}Vw​=Vπθ​, then

E[δt  |  st=s,at=a]=Aπθ(s,a).\mathbb{E} \left[ \delta_t \;\middle|\; s_t = s, a_t = a \right] = A^{\pi_\theta}(s,a).E[δt​∣st​=s,at​=a]=Aπθ​(s,a).

:::

::: proof Proof. Conditioning on st=s,at=as_t = s, a_t = ast​=s,at​=a,

E[δt∣st,at]=E[rt+γVπθ(st+1)−Vπθ(st)  |  st,at].\mathbb{E}[\delta_t \mid s_t,a_t] = \mathbb{E} \left[ r_t + \gamma V^{\pi_\theta}(s_{t+1}) - V^{\pi_\theta}(s_t) \;\middle|\; s_t,a_t \right].E[δt​∣st​,at​]=E[rt​+γVπθ​(st+1​)−Vπθ​(st​)∣st​,at​].

By the definition of the action-value function,

Qπθ(s,a)=E[rt+γVπθ(st+1)  |  st=s,at=a].Q^{\pi_\theta}(s,a)= \mathbb{E} \left[ r_t + \gamma V^{\pi_\theta}(s_{t+1}) \;\middle|\; s_t = s, a_t = a \right].Qπθ​(s,a)=E[rt​+γVπθ​(st+1​)∣st​=s,at​=a].

Substituting yields

E[δt∣st,at]=Qπθ(s,a)−Vπθ(s)=Aπθ(s,a).\mathbb{E}[\delta_t \mid s_t,a_t]= Q^{\pi_\theta}(s,a) - V^{\pi_\theta}(s) = A^{\pi_\theta}(s,a).E[δt​∣st​,at​]=Qπθ​(s,a)−Vπθ​(s)=Aπθ​(s,a).

 ◻ :::

Actor Update Using TD Error

Using the above result, the policy gradient may be written as

∇θJ(θ)=E[∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)Aπθ(st,at)]=E[∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st)E[δt∣st,at]].\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E} \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) A^{\pi_\theta}(s_t,a_t) \right] = \mathbb{E} \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \mathbb{E}[\delta_t \mid s_t,a_t] \right].∇θ​J(θ)=E[∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)Aπθ​(st​,at​)]=E[∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)E[δt​∣st​,at​]].

Exchanging expectations yields the actor update:

∇θJ(θ)=E[∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st) δt].\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E} \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\, \delta_t \right].∇θ​J(θ)=E[∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)δt​].

Bias Introduced by an Imperfect Critic

When Vw≠VπθV_w \neq V^{\pi_\theta}Vw​=Vπθ​, the TD error becomes a biased estimator of the advantage:

E[δt∣st,at]≠Aπθ(st,at).\mathbb{E}[\delta_t \mid s_t,a_t] \neq A^{\pi_\theta}(s_t,a_t).E[δt​∣st​,at​]=Aπθ​(st​,at​).

However, the variance reduction often outweighs this bias in practice.

Two-Time-Scale Stochastic Approximation

Actor-critic methods rely on two learning rates:

αw≫αθ.\alpha_w \gg \alpha_\theta.αw​≫αθ​.

The critic converges rapidly to a neighborhood of VπθV^{\pi_\theta}Vπθ​, while the actor evolves slowly.

Under standard assumptions on step sizes and function approximation, this scheme converges to a locally optimal policy.

Online and Incremental Updates

Actor-critic methods support fully online updates:

δt=rt+γVw(st+1)−Vw(st),w←w+αwδt∇wVw(st),θ←θ+αθδt∇θlog⁡πθ(at∣st).\begin{align*} \delta_t &= r_t + \gamma V_w(s_{t+1}) - V_w(s_t), \\ w &\leftarrow w + \alpha_w \delta_t \nabla_w V_w(s_t), \\ \theta &\leftarrow \theta + \alpha_\theta \delta_t \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t). \end{align*}δt​wθ​=rt​+γVw​(st+1​)−Vw​(st​),←w+αw​δt​∇w​Vw​(st​),←θ+αθ​δt​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​).​

These updates interleave policy improvement and policy evaluation at every step.

Interpretation

Actor-critic methods can be viewed as:

  • approximate policy iteration,

  • stochastic gradient ascent on J(θ)J(\theta)J(θ),

  • bootstrapped advantage estimation.

They form the backbone of modern deep reinforcement learning algorithms.

Limitations

Despite their success, actor-critic methods suffer from:

  • instability from critic misestimation,

  • sensitivity to learning rates,

  • bias from bootstrapping,

  • potential value function collapse.

These issues motivate trust-region methods and variance-controlled estimators, which we introduce next.

Discounted State Visitation and Performance Difference Lemma

Policy gradient methods optimize the expected return

J(π)=Eπ[∑t=0∞γtr(st,at)].J(\pi)= \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t r(s_t,a_t) \right].J(π)=Eπ​[t=0∑∞​γtr(st​,at​)].

To reason about finite policy updates and their effect on performance, we require a way to compare the value of two different policies. This comparison is formalized by the performance difference lemma.

Discounted State Visitation Distribution

We begin by defining the discounted state visitation distribution, which plays a central role in policy comparison.

::: definition Definition 16 (Discounted State Visitation Distribution). *Let π\piπ be a stationary policy. The discounted state visitation distribution induced by π\piπ is

dπ(s)=(1−γ)∑t=0∞γtPπ(st=s),s∈S.d^\pi(s) = (1-\gamma) \sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t \mathbb{P}_\pi(s_t = s), \qquad s \in \mathcal{S}.dπ(s)=(1−γ)t=0∑∞​γtPπ​(st​=s),s∈S.

:::

Properties of dπd^\pidπ

::: proposition Proposition 2. The distribution dπd^\pidπ satisfies:

  1. dπ(s)≥0d^\pi(s) \ge 0dπ(s)≥0 for all sss,

  2. ∑s∈Sdπ(s)=1\sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} d^\pi(s) = 1∑s∈S​dπ(s)=1,

  3. *For any bounded measurable function f:S→Rf : \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}f:S→R,

Es∼dπ[f(s)]=(1−γ)Eπ[∑t=0∞γtf(st)].\mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi}[f(s)] = (1-\gamma) \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t f(s_t) \right].Es∼dπ​[f(s)]=(1−γ)Eπ​[t=0∑∞​γtf(st​)].

:::

::: proof Proof. The claims follow directly from the definition and Fubini’s theorem. ◻ :::

Expected Return in Terms of dπd^\pidπ

The expected return can be rewritten as

J(π)=11−γEs∼dπ, a∼π(⋅∣s)[r(s,a)].J(\pi)= \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi,\, a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)} \left[ r(s,a) \right].J(π)=1−γ1​Es∼dπ,a∼π(⋅∣s)​[r(s,a)].

This representation highlights that J(π)J(\pi)J(π) depends on both the state visitation frequencies and the action distribution.

Action-Value Function Recap

Recall the definition of the action-value function:

Qπ(s,a)=Eπ[∑t=0∞γtr(st,at)  |  s0=s,a0=a].Q^\pi(s,a)= \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t r(s_t,a_t) \;\middle|\; s_0 = s, a_0 = a \right].Qπ(s,a)=Eπ​[t=0∑∞​γtr(st​,at​)​s0​=s,a0​=a].

The value function satisfies

Vπ(s)=Ea∼π(⋅∣s)[Qπ(s,a)].V^\pi(s)= \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)}[Q^\pi(s,a)].Vπ(s)=Ea∼π(⋅∣s)​[Qπ(s,a)].

Advantage Function Recap

The advantage function is defined as

Aπ(s,a)=Qπ(s,a)−Vπ(s).A^\pi(s,a)= Q^\pi(s,a) - V^\pi(s).Aπ(s,a)=Qπ(s,a)−Vπ(s).

It measures the relative value of taking action aaa at state sss compared to the average action under π\piπ.

Performance Difference Lemma

We now state the main result.

::: lemma Lemma 4 (Performance Difference Lemma). *Let π\piπ and π′\pi'π′ be two stationary policies. Then

J(π′)−J(π)=11−γEs∼dπ′[Ea∼π′(⋅∣s)[Aπ(s,a)]].J(\pi') - J(\pi)= \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^{\pi'}} \left[ \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi'(\cdot \mid s)} \left[ A^\pi(s,a) \right] \right].J(π′)−J(π)=1−γ1​Es∼dπ′​[Ea∼π′(⋅∣s)​[Aπ(s,a)]].

:::

Proof of the Performance Difference Lemma

::: proof Proof. Consider the quantity

Δt=γt(r(st,at)+γVπ(st+1)−Vπ(st)).\Delta_t = \gamma^t \left( r(s_t,a_t) + \gamma V^\pi(s_{t+1}) - V^\pi(s_t) \right).Δt​=γt(r(st​,at​)+γVπ(st+1​)−Vπ(st​)).

Taking expectations under π′\pi'π′,

Eπ′[Δt]=Eπ′[γtr(st,at)]+Eπ′[γt+1Vπ(st+1)−γtVπ(st)].\mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[\Delta_t]= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'} \left[ \gamma^t r(s_t,a_t) \right] + \mathbb{E}_{\pi'} \left[ \gamma^{t+1} V^\pi(s_{t+1}) - \gamma^t V^\pi(s_t) \right].Eπ′​[Δt​]=Eπ′​[γtr(st​,at​)]+Eπ′​[γt+1Vπ(st+1​)−γtVπ(st​)].

Summing over t≥0t \ge 0t≥0, the second term telescopes:

∑t=0∞Eπ′[γt+1Vπ(st+1)−γtVπ(st)]=−Es0∼ρ[Vπ(s0)].\sum_{t=0}^\infty \mathbb{E}_{\pi'} \left[ \gamma^{t+1} V^\pi(s_{t+1}) - \gamma^t V^\pi(s_t) \right]=- \mathbb{E}_{s_0 \sim \rho}[V^\pi(s_0)].t=0∑∞​Eπ′​[γt+1Vπ(st+1​)−γtVπ(st​)]=−Es0​∼ρ​[Vπ(s0​)].

Therefore,

∑t=0∞Eπ′[Δt]=J(π′)−J(π).\sum_{t=0}^\infty \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[\Delta_t]= J(\pi') - J(\pi).t=0∑∞​Eπ′​[Δt​]=J(π′)−J(π).

Now condition on sts_tst​:

Eπ′[Δt∣st]=γt(Ea∼π′(⋅∣st)[r(st,a)+γEs′∼P[Vπ(s′)]]−Vπ(st)).\mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[\Delta_t \mid s_t]= \gamma^t \left( \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi'(\cdot \mid s_t)} \left[ r(s_t,a) + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{s' \sim P}[V^\pi(s')] \right] - V^\pi(s_t) \right).Eπ′​[Δt​∣st​]=γt(Ea∼π′(⋅∣st​)​[r(st​,a)+γEs′∼P​[Vπ(s′)]]−Vπ(st​)).

By definition of the action-value function,

Ea∼π′[r(st,a)+γEs′Vπ(s′)]=Vπ(st)+Ea∼π′[Aπ(st,a)].\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi'} \left[ r(s_t,a) + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{s'} V^\pi(s') \right] = V^\pi(s_t) + \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi'}[A^\pi(s_t,a)].Ea∼π′​[r(st​,a)+γEs′​Vπ(s′)]=Vπ(st​)+Ea∼π′​[Aπ(st​,a)].

Thus,

Eπ′[Δt]=Eπ′[γtEa∼π′(⋅∣st)[Aπ(st,a)]].\mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[\Delta_t]= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'} \left[ \gamma^t \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi'(\cdot \mid s_t)} [A^\pi(s_t,a)] \right].Eπ′​[Δt​]=Eπ′​[γtEa∼π′(⋅∣st​)​[Aπ(st​,a)]].

Summing over ttt and using the definition of dπ′d^{\pi'}dπ′,

J(π′)−J(π)=11−γEs∼dπ′[Ea∼π′[Aπ(s,a)]].J(\pi') - J(\pi) = \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^{\pi'}} \left[ \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi'}[A^\pi(s,a)] \right].J(π′)−J(π)=1−γ1​Es∼dπ′​[Ea∼π′​[Aπ(s,a)]].

 ◻ :::

Interpretation

The performance difference lemma shows that:

  • Policy improvement depends on the expected advantage under the new policy,

  • The expectation is taken over the new policy’s state distribution,

  • Exact evaluation requires knowledge of dπ′d^{\pi'}dπ′.

This result is exact but not directly usable for optimization.

Implications for Policy Optimization

Since dπ′d^{\pi'}dπ′ depends on the new policy, direct optimization is intractable. Trust-region methods approximate dπ′d^{\pi'}dπ′ with dπd^\pidπ to obtain a tractable surrogate objective.

This approximation is the foundation of TRPO and PPO.

Trust Region Policy Optimization (TRPO)

Motivation

Vanilla policy gradient methods perform unconstrained gradient ascent on

J(πθ)=Eπθ[∑t=0∞γtr(st,at)].J(\pi_\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta}\Big[\sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t r(s_t,a_t)\Big].J(πθ​)=Eπθ​​[t=0∑∞​γtr(st​,at​)].

However, small changes in parameters θ\thetaθ can induce large changes in the policy distribution πθ(a∣s)\pi_\theta(a\mid s)πθ​(a∣s), potentially decreasing performance.

The Performance Difference Lemma motivates updating the policy conservatively:

J(π′)−J(π)=11−γEs∼dπ′[Ea∼π′[Aπ(s,a)]].J(\pi') - J(\pi) = \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^{\pi'}} \big[ \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi'}[A^\pi(s,a)] \big].J(π′)−J(π)=1−γ1​Es∼dπ′​[Ea∼π′​[Aπ(s,a)]].

Since the expectation is taken over dπ′d^{\pi'}dπ′, we cannot compute it exactly without running π′\pi'π′. TRPO addresses this by:

  1. Approximating dπ′≈dπd^{\pi'} \approx d^\pidπ′≈dπ for small policy changes,

  2. Adding a trust-region constraint to ensure π′\pi'π′ stays close to π\piπ.

Surrogate Objective

Define the surrogate objective based on the current policy π\piπ:

Lπ(π′):=Es∼dπ,a∼π′[Aπ(s,a)].L_\pi(\pi') := \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, a \sim \pi'}[A^\pi(s,a)].Lπ​(π′):=Es∼dπ,a∼π′​[Aπ(s,a)].

Using importance sampling, this can be rewritten as

Lπ(π′)=Es∼dπ,a∼π[π′(a∣s)π(a∣s)Aπ(s,a)].L_\pi(\pi') = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, a \sim \pi} \Big[ \frac{\pi'(a \mid s)}{\pi(a \mid s)} A^\pi(s,a) \Big].Lπ​(π′)=Es∼dπ,a∼π​[π(a∣s)π′(a∣s)​Aπ(s,a)].

This objective is tractable because it depends on dπd^\pidπ, which is known from trajectories sampled under π\piπ.

KL-Constrained Optimization Problem

To ensure that π′\pi'π′ does not deviate too far from π\piπ, TRPO imposes a constraint using the KL divergence:

::: definition Definition 17 (Average KL Divergence). *

DˉKL(π∥π′):=Es∼dπ[DKL(π(⋅∣s) ∥ π′(⋅∣s))].\bar{D}_{\mathrm{KL}}(\pi \| \pi') := \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi}\Big[ D_{\mathrm{KL}}(\pi(\cdot \mid s) \,\|\, \pi'(\cdot \mid s)) \Big].DˉKL​(π∥π′):=Es∼dπ​[DKL​(π(⋅∣s)∥π′(⋅∣s))].

:::

The TRPO optimization problem is:

max⁡π′Lπ(π′)s.t.DˉKL(π∥π′)≤δ,\begin{aligned} \max_{\pi'} & \quad L_\pi(\pi') \\ \text{s.t.} & \quad \bar{D}_{\mathrm{KL}}(\pi \| \pi') \le \delta, \end{aligned}π′max​s.t.​Lπ​(π′)DˉKL​(π∥π′)≤δ,​

where δ>0\delta > 0δ>0 is a small constant specifying the trust-region radius.

Linearization and Quadratic Approximation

Let πθ\pi_\thetaπθ​ be parameterized by θ\thetaθ. Linearizing the surrogate objective around θ0\theta_0θ0​ and using a second-order approximation for the KL constraint yields:

Lπ(πθ)≈Lπ(πθ0)+g⊤(θ−θ0),DˉKL(πθ0∥πθ)≈12(θ−θ0)⊤F(θ−θ0),L_\pi(\pi_\theta) \approx L_\pi(\pi_{\theta_0}) + g^\top (\theta - \theta_0), \quad \bar{D}_{\mathrm{KL}}(\pi_{\theta_0} \| \pi_\theta) \approx \frac{1}{2} (\theta - \theta_0)^\top F (\theta - \theta_0),Lπ​(πθ​)≈Lπ​(πθ0​​)+g⊤(θ−θ0​),DˉKL​(πθ0​​∥πθ​)≈21​(θ−θ0​)⊤F(θ−θ0​),

where

g=∇θLπ(πθ)∣θ0,F=Es∼dπ,a∼πθ[∇θlog⁡πθ(a∣s)∇θlog⁡πθ(a∣s)⊤]g = \nabla_\theta L_\pi(\pi_\theta)\big|_{\theta_0}, \quad F = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, a \sim \pi_\theta} \big[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)^\top \big]g=∇θ​Lπ​(πθ​)​θ0​​,F=Es∼dπ,a∼πθ​​[∇θ​logπθ​(a∣s)∇θ​logπθ​(a∣s)⊤]

is the Fisher information matrix.

The solution of the constrained optimization is the natural gradient update:

θk+1=θk+αF−1g,\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \alpha F^{-1} g,θk+1​=θk​+αF−1g,

where α\alphaα is chosen to satisfy the KL constraint.

Properties and Guarantees

  • TRPO guarantees monotonic improvement:
J(π′)≥J(π)−O(γ(1−γ)2δ),J(\pi') \ge J(\pi) - \mathcal{O}\Big(\frac{\gamma}{(1-\gamma)^2} \delta\Big),J(π′)≥J(π)−O((1−γ)2γ​δ),

provided δ\deltaδ is small.

  • By controlling the KL divergence, TRPO prevents catastrophic drops in performance.

  • The linearization and quadratic approximation make the update practical but require conjugate gradient methods and line search.

Limitations

Despite its theoretical appeal, TRPO has practical drawbacks:

  • Second-order optimization is computationally expensive,

  • Requires large batch sizes for accurate Fisher estimates,

  • Line search and conjugate gradient are complex to implement,

  • Scaling to large neural networks is difficult.

These limitations motivate the simpler Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), which we present next.

Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)

Motivation

While TRPO provides theoretical guarantees, it is computationally expensive due to:

  • Second-order optimization (Fisher matrix),

  • Conjugate gradient and line search,

  • Large batch requirements for stability.

PPO approximates TRPO with a first-order method that:

  1. Avoids explicit second-order computations,

  2. Maintains stability via a clipped surrogate objective,

  3. Can be efficiently implemented with standard stochastic gradient descent.

Probability Ratio and Surrogate Objective

Let πθold\pi_{\theta_{\mathrm{old}}}πθold​​ denote the current policy. Define the probability ratio:

rt(θ):=πθ(at∣st)πθold(at∣st).r_t(\theta) := \frac{\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)}{\pi_{\theta_{\mathrm{old}}}(a_t \mid s_t)}.rt​(θ):=πθold​​(at​∣st​)πθ​(at​∣st​)​.

The unclipped surrogate objective is:

LPG(θ):=E[rt(θ)A^t],L^{\mathrm{PG}}(\theta) := \mathbb{E}\big[ r_t(\theta) \hat{A}_t \big],LPG(θ):=E[rt​(θ)A^t​],

where A^t\hat{A}_tA^t​ is an estimator of the advantage function, e.g., Monte Carlo return, TD error, or GAE.

Clipped Objective

To prevent large policy updates that could destabilize training, PPO introduces a clipping mechanism:

::: definition Definition 18 (PPO Clipped Objective). *

LPPO(θ):=E[min⁡(rt(θ)A^t,  clip(rt(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A^t)],L^{\mathrm{PPO}}(\theta) := \mathbb{E} \Big[ \min \big( r_t(\theta) \hat{A}_t, \; \mathrm{clip}(r_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) \hat{A}_t \big) \Big],LPPO(θ):=E[min(rt​(θ)A^t​,clip(rt​(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A^t​)],

where ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0 is a hyperparameter controlling the maximum allowed deviation of rtr_trt​ from 111. :::

Intuition:

  • If A^t>0\hat{A}_t > 0A^t​>0, increasing rtr_trt​ beyond 1+ϵ1+\epsilon1+ϵ does not further increase the objective.

  • If A^t<0\hat{A}_t < 0A^t​<0, decreasing rtr_trt​ below 1−ϵ1-\epsilon1−ϵ does not further decrease the objective.

This enforces a soft trust region similar in spirit to TRPO, but in a first-order, easily computable manner.

Actor-Critic Variant with PPO

PPO is typically used in an actor-critic framework. Let Vw(s)V_w(s)Vw​(s) denote the critic parameterized by www.

Advantage Estimation

The Generalized Advantage Estimator (GAE) is commonly used:

δt=rt+γVw(st+1)−Vw(st),A^tGAE(γ,λ)=∑l=0∞(γλ)lδt+l.\delta_t = r_t + \gamma V_w(s_{t+1}) - V_w(s_t), \quad \hat{A}_t^{\mathrm{GAE}(\gamma,\lambda)} = \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} (\gamma \lambda)^l \delta_{t+l}.δt​=rt​+γVw​(st+1​)−Vw​(st​),A^tGAE(γ,λ)​=l=0∑∞​(γλ)lδt+l​.

Here, λ∈[0,1]\lambda \in [0,1]λ∈[0,1] controls the bias-variance tradeoff:

  • λ=0\lambda = 0λ=0: low-variance, one-step TD advantage,

  • λ=1\lambda = 1λ=1: unbiased, high-variance Monte Carlo advantage.

Actor Objective

The actor maximizes the PPO clipped objective using A^t=A^tGAE\hat{A}_t = \hat{A}_t^{\mathrm{GAE}}A^t​=A^tGAE​:

LPPO(θ)=E[min⁡(rt(θ)A^t,  clip(rt(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A^t)].L^{\mathrm{PPO}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E} \Big[ \min \big( r_t(\theta) \hat{A}_t, \; \mathrm{clip}(r_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) \hat{A}_t \big) \Big].LPPO(θ)=E[min(rt​(θ)A^t​,clip(rt​(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A^t​)].

Critic Objective

The critic minimizes the squared TD or Monte Carlo error:

Lvalue(w)=E[(Vw(st)−V^t)2],L_{\mathrm{value}}(w) = \mathbb{E} \big[ (V_w(s_t) - \hat{V}_t)^2 \big],Lvalue​(w)=E[(Vw​(st​)−V^t​)2],

where V^t\hat{V}_tV^t​ is a bootstrapped return:

V^t=∑l=0∞γlrt+lor using TD bootstrap: rt+γVw(st+1).\hat{V}_t = \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \gamma^l r_{t+l} \quad \text{or using TD bootstrap: } r_t + \gamma V_w(s_{t+1}).V^t​=l=0∑∞​γlrt+l​or using TD bootstrap: rt​+γVw​(st+1​).

Entropy Regularization

An entropy bonus is often added to encourage exploration:

Lentropy(θ)=E[H(πθ(⋅∣st))].L_{\mathrm{entropy}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E} \big[ \mathcal{H}(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s_t)) \big].Lentropy​(θ)=E[H(πθ​(⋅∣st​))].

Combined Objective

The full PPO actor-critic objective is:

L(θ,w)=LPPO(θ)−cvLvalue(w)+ceLentropy(θ),L(\theta, w) = L^{\mathrm{PPO}}(\theta) - c_v L_{\mathrm{value}}(w) + c_e L_{\mathrm{entropy}}(\theta),L(θ,w)=LPPO(θ)−cv​Lvalue​(w)+ce​Lentropy​(θ),

where cv,ce>0c_v, c_e > 0cv​,ce​>0 are coefficients.

Practical Implementation Notes

  • Mini-batch stochastic gradient descent or Adam optimizer is used.

  • Multiple epochs over the same batch can improve sample efficiency.

  • GAE hyperparameters (γ,λ)(\gamma, \lambda)(γ,λ) and clipping ϵ\epsilonϵ are tuned to balance stability and learning speed.

  • PPO provides empirical robustness close to TRPO without requiring second-order optimization or line search.

Summary

  • PPO approximates TRPO using a first-order, clipped surrogate objective.

  • Clipping ensures soft trust-region behavior, stabilizing updates.

  • Advantage estimation (e.g., GAE) and entropy regularization improve learning efficiency.

  • PPO is widely used in modern reinforcement learning due to its simplicity, scalability, and empirical performance.

Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)

Motivation

Large language models (LLMs) pretrained with maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) learn to model the distribution of text:

max⁡θEx∼D[∑t=1Tlog⁡pθ(xt∣x<t)].\max_\theta \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}} \Big[ \sum_{t=1}^T \log p_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}) \Big].θmax​Ex∼D​[t=1∑T​logpθ​(xt​∣x<t​)].

However, MLE does not explicitly optimize for:

  • Helpfulness,

  • Safety,

  • Alignment with human preferences.

RLHF introduces a learned reward function rϕ(x,y)r_\phi(x,y)rϕ​(x,y) that encodes human judgment and optimizes the policy to maximize this reward using reinforcement learning.

Three Stages of RLHF

RLHF proceeds in three stages:

  1. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): Learn an instruction-following policy πSFT\pi_{\mathrm{SFT}}πSFT​ using human demonstrations.

  2. Reward Model (RM) Training: Learn a reward model rϕ(x,y)r_\phi(x,y)rϕ​(x,y) from human preference comparisons.

  3. Policy Optimization: Optimize πθ\pi_\thetaπθ​ to maximize expected reward using PPO.

Stage I: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)

Given a dataset of prompt-response pairs (x,y∗)(x, y^*)(x,y∗), train the policy via maximum likelihood:

max⁡θ  E(x,y∗)[log⁡πθ(y∗∣x)].\max_\theta \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y^*)} [ \log \pi_\theta(y^* \mid x) ].θmax​E(x,y∗)​[logπθ​(y∗∣x)].

This yields an initial policy πSFT\pi_{\mathrm{SFT}}πSFT​ that:

  • Provides a reasonable starting point for RL,

  • Serves as a reference policy for KL regularization.

Stage II: Reward Model (RM) Training

Preference Data

Humans provide relative feedback in the form of comparisons:

(x,y+,y−),with y+≻y−.(x, y^+, y^-), \quad \text{with } y^+ \succ y^-.(x,y+,y−),with y+≻y−.

This avoids absolute score calibration and aligns training with comparative human judgments.

Reward Model Architecture

The reward model rϕ(x,y)r_\phi(x,y)rϕ​(x,y) is typically:

  • A transformer initialized from pretraining or SFT,

  • Augmented with a scalar output head representing latent human preference,

  • Fine-tuned on the preference dataset.

Reward Model Loss

Using a Bradley-Terry or logistic formulation:

LRM(ϕ)=−E(x,y+,y−)[log⁡σ(rϕ(x,y+)−rϕ(x,y−))],\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{RM}}(\phi) = - \mathbb{E}_{(x, y^+, y^-)} \Big[ \log \sigma(r_\phi(x, y^+) - r_\phi(x, y^-)) \Big],LRM​(ϕ)=−E(x,y+,y−)​[logσ(rϕ​(x,y+)−rϕ​(x,y−))],

where σ(z)=11+e−z\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}σ(z)=1+e−z1​ is the logistic sigmoid. This encourages rϕ(x,y+)>rϕ(x,y−)r_\phi(x, y^+) > r_\phi(x, y^-)rϕ​(x,y+)>rϕ​(x,y−).

Stage III: Policy Optimization with PPO

Treat rϕ(x,y)r_\phi(x,y)rϕ​(x,y) as a fixed reward function. Define the RL objective:

J(θ)=Ey∼πθ(⋅∣x)[rϕ(x,y)].J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{y \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x)}[ r_\phi(x, y) ].J(θ)=Ey∼πθ​(⋅∣x)​[rϕ​(x,y)].

Since rewards are non-differentiable with respect to discrete tokens, policy gradient methods (e.g., PPO) are used.

KL Regularization

To prevent policy drift away from the SFT distribution:

r~t=rϕ(xt,yt)−βDKL(πθ(⋅∣xt)∥πSFT(⋅∣xt)),\tilde{r}_t = r_\phi(x_t, y_t) - \beta D_{\mathrm{KL}} \big( \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x_t) \| \pi_{\mathrm{SFT}}(\cdot \mid x_t) \big),r~t​=rϕ​(xt​,yt​)−βDKL​(πθ​(⋅∣xt​)∥πSFT​(⋅∣xt​)),

with β>0\beta > 0β>0 controlling the strength of the penalty.

Advantage Estimation

Using a critic Vw(s)V_w(s)Vw​(s) and GAE:

δt=r~t+γVw(st+1)−Vw(st),A^t=∑l=0∞(γλ)lδt+l.\delta_t = \tilde{r}_t + \gamma V_w(s_{t+1}) - V_w(s_t), \quad \hat{A}_t = \sum_{l=0}^\infty (\gamma \lambda)^l \delta_{t+l}.δt​=r~t​+γVw​(st+1​)−Vw​(st​),A^t​=l=0∑∞​(γλ)lδt+l​.

Actor-Critic PPO Objective

L(θ,w)=E[min⁡(rt(θ)A^t,  clip(rt(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A^t)]−cvLvalue(w)+ceH(πθ).L(\theta, w) = \mathbb{E} \Big[ \min(r_t(\theta) \hat{A}_t, \; \mathrm{clip}(r_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) \hat{A}_t ) \Big] - c_v L_{\mathrm{value}}(w) + c_e \mathcal{H}(\pi_\theta).L(θ,w)=E[min(rt​(θ)A^t​,clip(rt​(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A^t​)]−cv​Lvalue​(w)+ce​H(πθ​).

Summary

  • Pretraining learns general language modeling,

  • SFT aligns the model to follow instructions,

  • Reward model captures human preferences via comparisons,

  • PPO optimizes the policy to maximize expected learned reward,

  • KL regularization ensures stability and prevents distribution shift.

Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)

Motivation

PPO-based RLHF relies on a learned critic to estimate advantages. However, this introduces:

  • Bias when the critic is misestimated,

  • Value function collapse in high-variance tasks,

  • Extra computational cost.

GRPO addresses these issues by replacing the critic with a group-relative baseline, leveraging multiple samples per prompt to compute relative advantages.

Group-Based Advantage

Given a prompt xxx, sample KKK completions:

{y(1),…,y(K)}∼πθ(⋅∣x).\{y^{(1)}, \dots, y^{(K)}\} \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x).{y(1),…,y(K)}∼πθ​(⋅∣x).

The reward model assigns scores:

R(k)=rϕ(x,y(k)),k=1,…,K.R^{(k)} = r_\phi(x, y^{(k)}), \quad k=1,\dots,K.R(k)=rϕ​(x,y(k)),k=1,…,K.

Define the group mean reward:

Rˉ=1K∑k=1KR(k).\bar{R} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^K R^{(k)}.Rˉ=K1​k=1∑K​R(k).

::: definition Definition 19 (GRPO Advantage). *The group-relative advantage is

A^(k)=R(k)−Rˉ.\hat{A}^{(k)} = R^{(k)} - \bar{R}.A^(k)=R(k)−Rˉ.

:::

Key Property

By construction:

Ek[A^(k)∣x]=0,\mathbb{E}_{k}[\hat{A}^{(k)} \mid x] = 0,Ek​[A^(k)∣x]=0,

so the baseline is unbiased, eliminating the need for a learned critic.

GRPO Objective

Define the probability ratio:

ρ(k)(θ)=πθ(y(k)∣x)πθold(y(k)∣x).\rho^{(k)}(\theta) = \frac{\pi_\theta(y^{(k)} \mid x)}{\pi_{\theta_{\mathrm{old}}}(y^{(k)} \mid x)}.ρ(k)(θ)=πθold​​(y(k)∣x)πθ​(y(k)∣x)​.

The GRPO objective mirrors PPO:

LGRPO(θ)=E[min⁡(ρ(k)(θ)A^(k),  clip(ρ(k)(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A^(k))].L^{\mathrm{GRPO}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E} \Big[ \min(\rho^{(k)}(\theta) \hat{A}^{(k)}, \; \mathrm{clip}(\rho^{(k)}(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) \hat{A}^{(k)}) \Big].LGRPO(θ)=E[min(ρ(k)(θ)A^(k),clip(ρ(k)(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A^(k))].

This removes the critic entirely and relies on relative group comparisons.

Advantages of GRPO

  • No critic required → avoids value estimation bias,

  • Variance reduction via within-group comparison,

  • Objective remains unbiased:

E[A^(k)]=0,\mathbb{E}[\hat{A}^{(k)}] = 0,E[A^(k)]=0,

  • Computationally simpler than PPO with a learned critic,

  • Naturally aligns with human preference comparisons (relative reward).

GRPO as PPO Without Critic

Conceptually, GRPO can be seen as:

::: center PPO where the value function V(s)V(s)V(s) is replaced by an empirical group mean Rˉ\bar{R}Rˉ. :::

This eliminates:

  • TD bootstrapping bias,

  • Critic collapse,

  • Instability from misestimated value functions.

Implementation Notes

  • Sample multiple completions per prompt (K≥4K \ge 4K≥4 typically),

  • Compute group-relative advantages and probability ratios,

  • Apply PPO clipping as usual,

  • No backpropagation through a critic is required.

Summary

  • GRPO simplifies RLHF by removing the critic,

  • Uses group-relative baselines to reduce variance,

  • Maintains unbiased policy gradient estimates,

  • Provides stable and computationally efficient training for large-scale language models.

GRPO workflow

GRPO Implementation for RLHF

The following pseudocode outlines the implementation of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) applied in the context of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). We assume access to a pretrained language model (e.g., GPT), a reward model trained on human preferences, and tokenized input/output sequences.

Setup

  • Actor (Policy) Model: Pretrained transformer language model πθ\pi_\thetaπθ​ producing token probabilities.

  • Reward Model: Pretrained or fine-tuned reward model rϕr_\phirϕ​ mapping sequences to scalar scores.

  • Data: Prompts xxx and model-generated completions yyy (candidate responses).

  • Groups: Partition of samples into groups G1,G2,…G_1, G_2, \dotsG1​,G2​,… for group-relative advantage computation.

Pytorch Pseudocode

Notes on Implementation

  • The policy model is typically a transformer (e.g., GPT) that outputs a probability distribution over the next token.

  • The reward model can be an MLP or transformer that outputs a scalar for a full sequence.

  • The group-relative baseline stabilizes training by centering rewards within each group, reducing variance without requiring a critic.

  • The clip operation enforces a soft trust region similar to PPO.

  • Sequences can be truncated or padded for efficient batching.

Conclusion

In this document, we have presented a rigorous and detailed derivation of policy gradient and actor—critic methods, highlighting the theoretical foundations and practical considerations for their implementation. Key contributions include:

  • The policy gradient theorem, including baseline and advantage function formulations,

  • Actor—critic methods and temporal-difference learning for variance reduction,

  • The performance difference lemma and its implications for trust-region optimization,

  • TRPO and PPO as methods for stable policy improvement in high-dimensional, parameterized policy spaces,

  • Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE) for bias—variance control in advantage estimation,

  • Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), including reward model training, KL regularization, and policy optimization,

  • Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) as a critic-free variant of PPO tailored to preference-based reward settings.

Through these derivations, we have connected formal theory with practical algorithm design, providing a pathway from foundational MDP concepts to modern reinforcement learning algorithms for large-scale language model alignment. The document is structured to enable both understanding and implementation, highlighting the interplay between mathematical rigor, variance reduction techniques, and algorithmic stability.

Future extensions of this framework could explore:

  • Alternative reward modeling strategies, such as uncertainty-aware or ensemble-based rewards,

  • Multi-agent or hierarchical extensions of GRPO for structured preference optimization,

  • Hybrid approaches combining direct preference optimization (DPO) with RLHF or GRPO.

Overall, this work situates modern policy optimization methods within a unified theoretical and practical framework, providing a foundation for both research and application in reinforcement learning and human-aligned AI.

Previous A Unified Mathematical Framework for Backpropagation, Attention, and State Management in Large-Scale Transformers
